lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Why, how now, Hecate! you look angerly.

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Have I not reason, beldams as you are,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Saucy and overbold? How did you dare

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: To trade and traffic with Macbeth

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: In riddles and affairs of death;

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: And I, the mistress of your charms,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: The close contriver of all harms,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Was never call’d to bear my part,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Or show the glory of our art?

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: And, which is worse, all you have done

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Hath been but for a wayward son,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Spiteful and wrathful, who, as others do,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Loves for his own ends, not for you.

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: But make amends now: get you gone,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: And at the pit of Acheron

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Meet me i’ the morning: thither he

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Will come to know his destiny:

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Your vessels and your spells provide,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Your charms and every thing beside.

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: I am for the air; this night I’ll spend

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Unto a dismal and a fatal end:

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Great business must be wrought ere noon:

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Upon the corner of the moon

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: There hangs a vaporous drop profound;

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: I’ll catch it ere it come to ground:

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: And that distill’d by magic sleights

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Shall raise such artificial sprites

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: As by the strength of their illusion

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Shall draw him on to his confusion:

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: He shall spurn fate, scorn death, and bear

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: He hopes ’bove wisdom, grace and fear:

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: And you all know, security

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Is mortals’ chiefest enemy.

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Hark! I am call’d; my little spirit, see,

lines.speaker: HECATE
lines.text: Sits in a foggy cloud, and stays for me.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Come, let’s make haste; she’ll soon be back again.

stage_directions.text: Thunder. Enter the three Witches meeting Hecate.
Music and a song within: “Come away, come away,” etc.

glosses.definition: Greek goddess of witchcraft and the moon; the Weird Sisters' mistress
the secret mastermind of all evils
Macbeth, the willful human client of the Witches
the pit of the underworld river Acheron, in Greek myth the river of woe
a river of the Greek underworld; river of woe
a drop of charmed vapor caught from the moon
overconfidence; false safety — the great enemy of the tragic protagonist

annotations.title: The Hecate scenes and the Middleton question
annotations.body:
3.5 is almost certainly not by Shakespeare. The scholarly consensus — dating to Edmond Malone in the 18th century and confirmed by computer-assisted attribution studies from Gary Taylor (Oxford) and Brian Vickers — identifies Thomas Middleton as the probable author of this scene and of parts of 4.1 (the songs 'Come away, come away' and 'Black spirits'). The scenes are interpolations added after 1609, probably for a revival of the play.

The evidence is stylistic. Hecate's speeches use tetrameter couplets in a way Shakespeare avoids in this play. The songs cited in 3.5 and 4.1 also appear in Middleton's *The Witch* (c. 1614). The internal incoherence — Hecate criticizes the Sisters for acting without her, but the rest of the play gives her no role before or after — suggests a late addition that never fully integrated.

Performance traditions split. Some productions cut Hecate entirely; others play her as a deliberate stylistic break, acknowledging the text's layered authorship. Reading *Macbeth* as a 'pure' Shakespeare play means reading past these scenes; reading it as the playtext it actually is means reading Middleton, too.

trials.prompt: Most scholars attribute 3.5 and parts of 4.1 to:
options: Thomas Middleton — an interpolation added after 1609, probably for a revival
The Sisters have acted without her — traded prophecies with Macbeth unsupervised
